perturbation.original_text: All notices given under this Agreement must be in writing. A notice is effective upon receipt and shall be sent via one of the following methods: delivery in person, overnight courier service, certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, addressed to the party to be notified at the below address or by facsimile at the below facsimile number or in the case of either party, to such other party, address or facsimile number as such party may designate upon reasonable notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices given under this Agreement must be in writing. A notice is effective upon receipt and shall be sent via one of the following methods: delivery in person, overnight courier service, certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, addressed to the party to be notified at the below address or by facsimile at the below facsimile number or in the case of either party, to such other party, address or facsimile number as such party may designate upon reasonable notice to the other party. In certain situations, oral notices shall be acceptable in place of a written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a sentence saying 'In certain situations, oral notices shall be acceptable in place of a written notice' after stating that all notices must be in writing introduces a contradiction, making it unclear whether written notices are strictly required, potentially conflicting with contract law principles of clear agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The statute requires a written agreement for contracts for the sale of goods. By allowing an additional line for 'oral' agreements it directly contradicts the need for written documentation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.2-201 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-201 336.2-107 336.2-202 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-201 Version List Recent History 2024 336.2-201 Amended 2024 c 93 art 2 s 3 336.2-201 FORMAL REQUIREMENTS; STATUTE OF FRAUDS. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is a record sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by the party's authorized agent or broker. A record is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this subsection beyond the quantity of goods shown in the record. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a record in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against the party unless notice in a record of objection to its contents is given within ten days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that all notices must be in writing, while the changed text introduces an exception allowing oral notices in certain situations. This directly contradicts the initial requirement for written notices. The Law Explanation indicates that the statute requires a written agreement for the sale of goods, and by allowing oral agreements, the changed text directly contradicts the need for written documentation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that a contract for the sale of goods for $500 or more is not enforceable unless there is a record (i.e., written documentation). The addition of 'oral notices' weakens the original intent and creates a contradiction, since oral agreements do not leave a paper trail, making them more susceptible to errors. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction since the organization of the contract now affects its clarity and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that use or disclosure of any Confidential Information in a manner inconsistent with this Agreement will give rise to irreparable injury for which: (a) money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by such party; (b) the other party may be entitled to specific performance and injunction and other equitable relief with respect to any such breach; (c) such remedies will not be the exclusive remedies for any such breach, but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity; and (d) in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final non-appealable order that one party, or any of its Representatives, has breached this Agreement, such party will be liable for reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred by the other party in connection with such litigation, including, but not limited to, any appeals.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that use or disclosure of any Confidential Information in a manner inconsistent with this Agreement will give rise to irreparable injury for which: (a) money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by such party; (b) the other party may be entitled to specific performance and injunction and other equitable relief with respect to any such breach; (c) such remedies will not be the exclusive remedies for any such breach, but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity; and (d) in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final non-appealable order that one party, or any of its Representatives, has breached this Agreement, such party will be liable for reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred by the other party in connection with such litigation, including, but not limited to, any appeals. However, any breaches to this agreement will be capped at $500, regardless of damages.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'However, any breaches to this agreement will be capped at $500, regardless of damages', there is a monetary cap put in place, restricting the ability to be provided money damages. As written, there is an understanding that monetary damages 'may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach', however there is a cap on the breach, making the availability of damages very restricted.
perturbation.law_explanation: The law addresses misappropriation of trade secrets. While parties can contractually agree on remedies, setting an unreasonably low cap ($500) regardless of actual damages could be viewed as undermining the purpose of the statute to protect trade secrets, especially if the actual losses due to misappropriation are far greater. This may conflict with the legal intent behind preventing the acquisition of trade secrets through improper means. The damages may not fit the intended loss.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.03 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.03 325C.02 325C.04 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.03 Version List 325C.03 DAMAGES. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under paragraph (a). History: 1980 c 594 s 5 ; 1987 c 1 s 2 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a monetary cap of $500 on all breaches, regardless of damages. The law snippet (Sec. 325C.03 MN Statutes) states that a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation, including actual loss and unjust enrichment. By capping damages at $500, the modified text directly contradicts the law's provision for recovering actual losses, as the actual losses could significantly exceed $500. The cap undermines the ability to recover full damages as intended by the law.
